One-line summary: get Eden installed and ready to open — answer two clicks to find the exact download for your device, then follow the drag-and-drop steps for your system (Linux gets one-click updates through Gear Lever). This guide covers getting Eden installed and running. Configuring it (keys, games, graphics settings) comes in a later section. You don't need to be technical to follow this. There are no commands to memorize — where a command is needed, you can copy and paste it exactly as shown, and the rest is clicking and dragging files.
documents.s: Set up Eden — Nintendo Switch emulator

documents.t: Eden is an open-source Nintendo Switch emulator — a program that runs Switch software on your PC. It's a continuation of the lineage of earlier Switch emulators, and it's distributed as a self-contained download you point at your own games rather than something you install from an app store. A few things to know up front: There are several versions to download. Eden offers a few different downloads, each tuned for a different kind of computer (a normal PC, a Steam Deck, a handheld like the ROG Ally, and so on). Don't worry about which is which — the picker in step 1 asks you two simple questions and tells you exactly which one to get. You bring your own games and keys. Eden is just the emulator; it does not come with any Nintendo games. Getting your games and keys onto it is covered in the setup section later. On Linux it's an AppImage. An AppImage is a single file that contains the whole app — there's nothing to install, you just run the file. To make it feel like a normal installed app (with a menu icon and an update button), we'll add it to a free helper app called Gear Lever.

You'll need a 64-bit computer made in the last decade or so — almost any PC, laptop, Steam Deck, or gaming handheld qualifies. You don't need to know your exact processor model; the picker in step 1 figures out the right download from a couple of plain-English questions. Pick your system below to see if there's anything to install first: Linux macOS Windows On Linux we'll install Eden through Gear Lever — a small, free helper app that takes the downloaded file and turns it into a normal installed app with a menu icon and, best of all, an update button so you never have to re-download Eden by hand. Install Gear Lever by copying and pasting this command into a terminal: flatpak install flathub it.mijorus.gearlever Don't have Flatpak? The command above uses Flatpak, an app installer that most Linux systems include. If your computer doesn't recognize the flatpak command, install it from your system's software/package manager first, then run this once to connect it to the Flathub app store: flatpak remote-add --if-not-exists flathub https://flathub.org/repo/flathub.flatpakrepo Nothing to install first — on a Mac, Eden comes as a .dmg file that you open and drag into your Applications folder, just like most Mac apps. Mac support is experimental The Mac version is still experimental — expect graphical glitches and crashes. If you also have a Windows PC or a Linux machine, Eden will run much more smoothly there. Nothing to install first — on Windows, Eden comes as a .zip file that you unzip and run. Windows can open zip files on its own, so you don't need any extra software (though 7-Zip works too if you prefer it).

Eden offers a few different downloads, and grabbing the right one matters — the wrong one can run slowly, crash, or not start at all. Instead of making you read through hardware charts, the picker below does it for you. Click your system, then click the option that best describes your device, and it'll tell you the exact file to download and hand you a link to the downloads page. 1 What are you installing Eden on? WindowsMacLinux What's "PGO"? You'll see most downloads come in a Standard and a PGO version. PGO is just a faster, more optimized version of the same thing — always try it first. The only reason to pick Standard is if the PGO one ever misbehaves. The picker already points you at the right one. If you'd rather choose for yourself — or you're just curious what all the options are — the full list is here: See every download and exactly who it's for Every option also comes in a Standard and a PGO version (except where noted); prefer PGO unless it misbehaves, then fall back to Standard. Linux macOS Windows Linux builds are AppImages. Build Use it if… amd64 Generic x86-64 — the default choice for most desktops/laptops. Legacy amd64 Pre-Ryzen / pre-Haswell CPUs. Older hardware; expect bugs. Steam Deck Steam Deck (Zen 2). Zen 4 AMD Z1/Z2 handhelds — ROG Ally X, Legion Go S. ARM (aarch64) 64-bit ARM machines. There's a single option: macOS DMG. (See the experimental warning in Prerequisites.) Windows builds come as zips. Build Use it if… Versions amd64/x86_64 (MSVC) You hit graphical issues on the other builds (e.g. Pokémon Scarlet & Violet). The compatibility fallback. MSVC zip only — no PGO amd64/x86_64 v3 (MinGW) Ryzen, 4th-gen Intel, or newer — the default for most modern PCs. Standard + PGO Zen 4 Zen 4 or newer (ROG Ally X, Legion Go S). Not compatible with Intel. Standard + PGO aarch64/arm64 Snapdragon (ARM) devices. Standard + PGO Once you've downloaded the file (it lands in your Downloads folder by default), come back here and continue to step 2.

Now that you've got the download, let's turn it into an app you can open. The steps are different for each system, so pick your tab. Linux macOS Windows We'll add the downloaded file to Gear Lever (the helper you installed earlier). Gear Lever tucks Eden away neatly, gives it a menu icon, and lets you update it later with a single button — no re-downloading by hand. Open Gear Lever. You'll find it in your applications menu, the same place your other apps live. Open your file manager and find the Eden file you just downloaded. It's in your Downloads folder and its name ends in .AppImage. Drag that file from your file manager onto the Gear Lever window, and let go. Gear Lever will show you the app, ready to add. If a button looks locked or greyed out Look for an unlock button in the top-right corner and click it — that gives Gear Lever permission to set the file up. (Depending on your system you may not see this at all, which is fine.) Click the button to add / install the app. That's it — Eden now appears in your applications menu like any other program, and you can launch it from there. To double-check that future updates will work: click Eden inside Gear Lever and scroll down to the Update Management section. As long as the Update URL box has a web address filled in, you're set — that's how Gear Lever knows where to look for new versions. It'll say no updates available until Eden actually releases a newer one, which is exactly what you want to see right now. Double-click the downloaded .dmg file to open it. A small window pops up showing the Eden app. Drag the Eden icon onto the Applications folder shown in that window. Then you can close the window and eject the disk image (drag it to the Trash or click the eject arrow next to it). Open Eden from your Applications folder. The first time, macOS will likely block it because it's not from the App Store. To allow it, go to menu → System Settings → Privacy & Security, scroll to the Security section, and click Allow (sometimes worded Allow untrusted app). Then open Eden again and it'll start. There's no installer on Windows — you just unzip the download and run the program inside. Find the .zip file you downloaded (it's in your Downloads folder). Right-click it → Extract All…, and choose a permanent home for it, such as a new folder called Eden in your Documents. Avoid leaving it inside Downloads, since you might clear that folder later. Don't run Eden from inside the zip If you double-click into the zip and run Eden without extracting first, it may fail or behave oddly. Always Extract All to a real folder first. Open the folder you extracted to and double-click eden.exe to start Eden. The first time, Windows may show a blue "Windows protected your PC" box. This is normal for apps it hasn't seen before — click More info, then Run anyway. Updating later on Windows The Windows version doesn't update itself. When a new version comes out, download the new zip and extract it over the same folder, replacing the old files. Open Eden for the first time. You should see its main window with an empty list of games — that's exactly right at this stage, because we haven't added keys or games yet. As long as the window opens and doesn't immediately crash, the download you picked is correct and you're ready to set it up.

Switch games are encrypted, so Eden needs two things before it can run anything: a set of keys (a small file called prod.keys that unlocks the games) and the Switch firmware (the console's system software). Eden ships with neither, because they're Nintendo's property — on a real setup you dump them from a Switch you own. The keys and firmware versions must match This is the single most important rule in this section: your prod.keys and your firmware have to be the same version as each other. A mismatch is the most common reason installs fail or games refuse to boot. If you grab one, grab the matching version of the other. Download the keys. Go to the prod.keys download page and scroll down a little until you reach the table of versions. Pick a version and download it — and note exactly which version number it is, because the firmware has to match it. Download the matching firmware. Go to the firmware download page, scroll down a little, and use the download button to grab the same version you just picked for the keys. Unzip the keys, but leave the firmware zipped. The keys come in a zip — extract it so you end up with the raw files inside: prod.keys and title.keys. You can delete title.keys; Eden doesn't need it here. Keep prod.keys. Do not unzip the firmware — Eden wants the firmware as the .zip file exactly as you downloaded it. Install the keys in Eden. In Eden's top menu, click Tools → Install Decryption Keys, then select your prod.keys file. Install the firmware in Eden. Again from the top menu, click Tools → Install Firmware → From ZIP, then select the firmware zip file. Both should finish without any error. If you get an error here, it's almost always a version mismatch — re-check that the keys and firmware are the same version (see the warning above) and try again.

Before you download a single game, set up a tidy folder structure — this isn't optional housekeeping, it's required. Eden treats base games and their updates/DLC differently, and they cannot live in the same folder. If you mix them, Eden won't pick them up correctly. Create a structure like this somewhere convenient (ROOT_DIR is just a folder of your choosing, e.g. in your Documents): ROOT_DIR/ └── GameFiles/ ├── games/ ← base game files only │ └── Mario Kart 8 Deluxe/ │ └── game-file.nsp └── DLC & Updates/ ← updates and DLC only └── Mario Kart 8 Deluxe/ ├── update-file.nsp └── dlc-file.nsp The key idea: the one base game file goes under games/, and everything else for that game (its update and any DLC) goes under DLC & Updates/. Using a per-game subfolder inside each (like Mario Kart 8 Deluxe/) keeps things readable once you have a few titles.

Game-download sites are heavy with ads and fake buttons, so a little caution goes a long way. Install an ad blocker first Before visiting any download site, install an ad blocker — uBlock Origin is the recommended one. It hides the pop-ups these sites are covered in, so you click the real link instead of malware. Real Switch games are only .nsp or .xci files Genuine Switch game files only ever come as .nsp or .xci. If a "game" downloads as a .exe, .msi, or anything that asks you to run an installer, it is malware — delete it immediately. No real game needs you to run a program to install it. A good source is nswpedia.com. For example, Mario Kart 8 Deluxe lives at its nswpedia page. As you download, sort the files into the folders from step 4 right away: the single base game file goes into games/, and any update or DLC files go into DLC & Updates/. Getting this right now saves you confusion later.

With your files sorted, point Eden at them. There are two folders to add — one for games, one for updates/DLC — and it matters that you pick the right one each time. Add the games folder. In Eden's main window, click the big add game directory button in the middle of the screen, and select your ROOT_DIR/GameFiles/games folder. Pick games, not GameFiles Select the inner games folder specifically — not the parent GameFiles folder. If you point Eden at GameFiles, it'll sweep up your updates and DLC as if they were games and things will misbehave. Add the updates/DLC folder. From the top menu, click Emulation → Configure. Find the External Content section, click Add Directory, and select your ROOT_DIR/GameFiles/DLC & Updates folder. Save. Your game should now appear in Eden's list, with its update and DLC applied automatically.

By default your in-game profile is just called Eden, and some games show it — Mario Kart, for instance, uses it as your player name. To change it, go to Emulation → Configure, and in the popup open System → Profiles. Right-click your profile and choose to edit it, then set the name you want. Profiles hold your save data Game progress (saves) is stored per profile, so if you delete a profile its save data goes with it. Renaming is safe; deleting is not.

Make sure your controller is plugged in or connected first, then go to Emulation → Configure and open the Controls section. By default no controller is selected, so pick your controller from the list — most of the remaining settings are fine left at their defaults. Two things are worth changing or checking before you play: Fix the face buttons. Look at the Face Buttons group in the top-right. By default Eden has X/Y and A/B swapped compared to where your fingers expect them, which feels wrong in most games. Remap them so they match your controller's physical layout. Keep console mode on "Docked". At the bottom-left, make sure console mode is set to Docked. It's the default, but it's worth confirming you haven't changed it — docked mode gives better image quality and performance than handheld mode.

Back on Eden's main screen you should now see your game in the list (with its box art once it loads). Double-click it to launch. If the game boots and reaches its title screen, everything is set up correctly — keys, firmware, and folders are all working together. You're ready to play.

Eden won't start, or it crashes the moment you open it (Linux or Windows): you most likely have the wrong download for your device. Go back to the picker in step 1 and try a safer choice — on Linux the plain amd64 download (or Legacy amd64 if your PC is quite old), and on Windows the amd64/x86_64 (MSVC) download, which is the most compatible one. A game looks glitchy on Windows (for example Pokémon Scarlet & Violet): switch to the amd64/x86_64 (MSVC) download — it exists specifically to fix graphics problems the other versions sometimes have. You downloaded the PGO version and it's acting up: download the Standard version of the same option instead. They install the exact same way. Linux — Gear Lever's Update URL box is empty: remove Eden from Gear Lever and add the file again. If it's still empty, you may have downloaded from the wrong place — get the file straight from the official downloads page using the picker above. Mac — "Eden can't be opened" or "unidentified developer": this is macOS being cautious. Open menu → System Settings → Privacy & Security, scroll to Security, click Allow, then open Eden again. Installing keys or firmware fails with an error: almost always a version mismatch. Make sure your prod.keys and firmware are the same version and re-install both. Also confirm you picked the unzipped prod.keys file for the keys, and the still-zipped .zip for the firmware. Your game doesn't show up in Eden's list: check it's a .nsp or .xci file sitting in the games folder (not GameFiles, and not the DLC & Updates folder), then in Eden refresh/reload the games list. The game shows up but updates or DLC aren't applied: the update/DLC files need to be in the DLC & Updates folder that you added under Emulation → Configure → External Content — not in the games folder. A game won't boot or crashes at the title screen: double-check keys and firmware installed without error (version match again), and that you grabbed a real .nsp/.xci rather than a fake file.
Eden releases page Eden website Gear Lever (Flathub) What is an AppImage? prod.keys download · Switch firmware download uBlock Origin (ad blocker) nswpedia (game files)

One-line summary: get the XXMI Launcher installed and injecting mods into the game of your choice, whether you launch it through Steam or through the HoyoPlay launcher (run with Heroic on Linux). This guide is written primarily for Linux, where getting mods to inject under Proton is the fiddly part — Windows is comparatively painless and is covered in shorter notes where it differs. The flow is the same for every game XXMI supports; we work it through with Zenless Zone Zero and Wuthering Waves because those are the two verified here, and call out the small per-game differences (mainly Wuthering Waves) as we go.
documents.s: Set up the XXMI Launcher to mod games

documents.t: XXMI Launcher is an open-source modding platform for HoYoverse games and Wuthering Waves. A model importer is the piece that actually loads your mods into a running game by hooking its graphics — XXMI bundles one importer per game under a single launcher, so you install and update them all from one place: Importer Game GIMI Genshin Impact SRMI Honkai: Star Rail ZZMI Zenless Zone Zero WWMI Wuthering Waves HIMI Honkai Impact 3rd EFMI Endfield What XXMI does for you: Downloads and auto-updates the right importer for each game. Injects the mod loader (d3d11.dll) when the game launches, so the game itself loads your mods. Acts as a drop-in launcher — you point your existing launcher (Steam, Heroic) at XXMI instead of the game, and XXMI starts the game for you with mods active. The rest of this guide is about wiring that last point up correctly on Linux, which is where most people get stuck. Modding online games carries a ban risk These are live online games. Mods like XXMI are widely used and generally considered low-risk because they only alter what you see locally, but there is no official guarantee — you use them at your own risk. On Linux, which Proton build you choose also carries a ban risk — see the warning under Prerequisites before you play.

The game already installed — through Steam, or through the HoyoPlay launcher in a folder you know the path to. (Installing the games themselves is a separate guide.) XXMI Launcher — we download it in step 1. Linux Windows You need a recent Proton build — GE-Proton or DW-Proton 11.0-5 or newer. These games are picky about Proton, so being able to switch versions quickly is worth it. Choose your Proton build with ban risk in mind There are warnings circulating that using a Proton version other than Proton-GE or Proton-Cachy carries a chance of being banned. The author has not been banned using DW-Proton, but treat this as unverified — prefer Proton-GE or Proton-Cachy wherever a build works for you, and only reach for others (like DW-Proton) if those don't. The easiest way to install and manage Proton versions is ProtonPlus from Flathub — it installs custom Proton builds into the right place for both Steam and Heroic to find them: flatpak install flathub com.vysp3r.ProtonPlus Don't have Flatpak? Install it from your distro's package manager first, then connect it to Flathub: flatpak remote-add --if-not-exists flathub https://flathub.org/repo/flathub.flatpakrepo For the HoyoPlay-via-Heroic route you'll also want Heroic Games Launcher installed. For the Steam route, the regular Steam client is all you need. Nothing extra to install up front. If you use the portable build of XXMI (step 1) you'll need the Visual C++ Redistributable — most gaming PCs already have it, and the installer build pulls it in for you, so on Windows the installer is the simplest path.

Grab XXMI from the releases page. There are two builds and which one you want depends on your OS. Linux Windows Download the portable version (the .zip, not the .msi). On Linux the portable build is required — the Windows installer expects system integration that doesn't exist under Proton/Wine. Extract it somewhere permanent that you'll remember the path to (not your Downloads folder, which you might clear later). The portable build auto-updates itself, so once it's set up you never have to re-download it by hand. Rename the extracted folder to exactly XXMI Launcher. It unzips with a versioned name like XXMI-Launcher-Portable-vX.X.X, but every path in this guide — and the launch options later — expect a folder named XXMI Launcher. Rename it now and the rest just works: mv "XXMI-Launcher-Portable-vX.X.X" "XXMI Launcher" Get this name exactly right The folder must be XXMI Launcher — capital Xs, a normal space, no version suffix — because paths like XXMI Launcher/Resources/Bin/ depend on it. A single extra space, or a wrong capital letter, breaks injection silently and is genuinely painful to debug. Rename only this top folder; leave everything inside it (Resources, Bin, …) exactly as it came. Download the installer (XXMI-Launcher-Installer-Online-vX.X.X.msi) and run it. Choose Quick Installation for the default location (%AppData%\XXMI Launcher) or Custom Installation to pick your own folder. When it finishes, the XXMI Launcher window opens on its own. The portable .zip also works on Windows if you prefer it (extract it outside Program Files and install the Visual C++ Redistributable first), but the installer is the path of least resistance here.

Linux Windows For the portable build, the executable lives at: XXMI Launcher/Resources/Bin/XXMI Launcher.exe Run that .exe under Wine — we just need its window to open so we can configure the importer. The Steam and Heroic wiring that makes it launch the game comes in step 5. The installer launches XXMI for you when it's done. If you used the portable build, run XXMI Launcher/Resources/Bin/XXMI Launcher.exe.

In the XXMI window, select your game and start its importer download (for Zenless Zone Zero that's ZZMI; for Wuthering Waves, WWMI). It's very common for this download to fail or stall — XXMI pulls files from GitHub, and GitHub rate-limits anonymous requests, so you'll often hit a wall. The fix is to give XXMI a GitHub token (a personal access key that raises the rate limit). You need a free GitHub account for this. Click the cogwheel (top-right) to open settings, then go to the Launcher tab. In the GitHub token field, click Create. That sends you to GitHub to make a classic personal access token. Create the token with the public_repo scope enabled. Set the expiration to whatever you like — a longer expiry just means you won't have to redo this as soon. Copy the token and paste it into the GitHub Token field in XXMI. Copy the token immediately GitHub only shows a token once. If you navigate away before copying it, you'll have to delete it and generate a new one. Retry the importer download. With the token in place it should now complete.
documents.s: 3. Set up the model importer (and the GitHub-token workaround)

documents.t: XXMI needs to know where the game is installed so it can inject into it. Open the cogwheel settings again, stay on the General tab, and browse to your game folder. The folder XXMI wants is the one that directly contains the game's main executable — pick the wrong level and injection won't find the game. For Zenless Zone Zero that's the folder holding ZenlessZoneZero.exe. Where that folder is depends on how you installed the game: Steam HoyoPlay launcher Under your Steam library. For Zenless Zone Zero, for example: [steam games folder]/SteamLibrary/steamapps/common/Zenless Zone Zero/games/ZenlessZoneZero Game/ ([steam games folder] is wherever your Steam library lives — by default ~/.steam/steam or ~/.local/share/Steam on Linux, but it may be on another drive if you set up a separate library.) Other games sit under their own common/<Game Name>/ folder in the same way. If you installed via the HoyoPlay launcher into a custom location, it's just the game's folder inside it. For Zenless Zone Zero: [install location]/ZenlessZoneZero Game/ For example, on the author's machine that's /mnt/Gaming/Gacha/ZenlessZoneZero Game/.
documents.s: 4. Point the importer at your game folder

documents.t: If you press Play now, it fails with an error like: ZZMI Loading Failed Library {your XXMI folder}/XXMI Launcher/ZZMI/d3d11.dll is missing expected entry point This happens because the mod loader (d3d11.dll) has to sit next to the game and be loaded with it, but XXMI lives in its own folder. The fix is to put XXMI inside the game folder and tell your launcher to start XXMI instead of the game. The exact steps differ a little by game and launcher. On Windows this step is unnecessary The native XXMI install handles injection itself — just press Play in the XXMI window. The rest of this step is the Linux-under-Proton fix. Pick which game you're modding, then how you launch it — the steps are tailored to each. Zenless Zone Zero Wuthering Waves Steam HoyoPlay launcher (via Heroic) 1. Put XXMI in the game folder. Move or symlink your XXMI Launcher folder into the game's install folder, common/Zenless Zone Zero/. A symbolic link keeps a single copy and is tidiest, but there are reports of symlinks causing injection to fail — if it doesn't work, move the folder in instead. To create a symlink (adjust both paths to yours): ln -s "/path/to/XXMI Launcher" "/path/to/SteamLibrary/steamapps/common/Zenless Zone Zero/XXMI Launcher" 2. Set the Steam launch options. Right-click the game in Steam → Properties → Launch Options, and paste: cmd=(%command%); cmd[-1]="$PWD/XXMI Launcher/Resources/Bin/XXMI Launcher.exe"; "${cmd[@]}" #--nogui --xxmi ZZMI This rewrites Steam's launch command to start the XXMI launcher (sitting in the game folder) instead of the game directly. 3. Press Play. The XXMI launcher window should appear; click its Play button and the game should boot with mods active. 4. Hide the launcher (once it works). Remove the leading # so the tail becomes --nogui --xxmi ZZMI. Those flags tell XXMI to skip its own window and launch the importer straight away, so you go directly into the game in future: cmd=(%command%); cmd[-1]="$PWD/XXMI Launcher/Resources/Bin/XXMI Launcher.exe"; "${cmd[@]}" --nogui --xxmi ZZMI 1. Put XXMI in the game folder. Copy or symlink your XXMI Launcher folder into the game's folder, [install location]/ZenlessZoneZero Game/. 2. Point Heroic at XXMI. In Heroic, add a game (or edit your existing entry) so its executable is the XXMI launcher inside that folder: [install location]/ZenlessZoneZero Game/XXMI Launcher/Resources/Bin/XXMI Launcher.exe 3. Set the Proton version to DW-Proton 11.0-5. ZZZ is fussy here. Under the game's settings, choose DW-Proton 11.0-5. If it doesn't work, try other versions — for the author, GE-Proton does not work for the HoyoPlay route, so don't be surprised if you have to experiment. 4. Enable "Disable umu". In the game's settings, make sure Disable umu is turned on — the launch fails without it. 5. Launch. Once the game starts correctly, add the launch flags under Settings → Advanced → Game Arguments to skip the XXMI window in future: --nogui --xxmi ZZMI Wuthering Waves needs two extra things beyond the basic flow: a SteamOS=1 in the launch options, and an extra DLL copied into the game. 1. Put XXMI in the game folder. Move or symlink your XXMI Launcher folder into the game's install folder, common/Wuthering Waves/. Prefer a symlink; if injection fails, move the folder in instead: ln -s "/path/to/XXMI Launcher" "/path/to/SteamLibrary/steamapps/common/Wuthering Waves/XXMI Launcher" 2. Copy the extra DLL. Copy d3dcompiler_47.dll from your WWMI folder into the game's binary folder, next to Client-Win64-Shipping.exe: Wuthering Waves/Client/Binaries/Win64/ 3. Set the Steam launch options. Right-click the game → Properties → Launch Options and paste this — note the added SteamOS=1 and the WWMI importer: cmd=(%command%); cmd[-1]="$PWD/XXMI Launcher/Resources/Bin/XXMI Launcher.exe"; SteamOS=1 "${cmd[@]}" #--nogui --xxmi WWMI 4. Press Play. The XXMI launcher window should appear; click its Play button and the game should boot with mods active. 5. Hide the launcher (once it works). Remove the leading # so the tail becomes --nogui --xxmi WWMI, and you'll go straight into the game in future: cmd=(%command%); cmd[-1]="$PWD/XXMI Launcher/Resources/Bin/XXMI Launcher.exe"; SteamOS=1 "${cmd[@]}" --nogui --xxmi WWMI

Launch the game the way you set up above. Success looks like: The game boots to the title screen with no … Loading Failed error. If you have a mod installed, its change is visible in-game; otherwise the importer's on-screen overlay (toggled with the keys it shows) confirms the loader is active. If you removed the # (Steam) or added the flags (Heroic), the game should go straight in without the XXMI window appearing.

The same launcher mods every game in the table at the top — GIMI (Genshin), SRMI (Star Rail), HIMI (Honkai Impact), EFMI (Endfield). The loop is identical: select the game in XXMI, download its importer, point it at the game's folder, and apply the Linux launch fix from step 5 — wherever those steps say ZZMI, use that game's importer instead (e.g. GIMI for Genshin). They're not all individually verified here, so if one misbehaves, the troubleshooting below and a different Proton version are the first things to try.

… Loading Failed … d3d11.dll is missing expected entry point: XXMI isn't being launched in place of the game. Re-check step 5 — XXMI must be inside the game folder, and the launch options / Heroic executable must point at XXMI Launcher/Resources/Bin/XXMI Launcher.exe. Importer download fails or hangs: add a GitHub token (step 3). Anonymous GitHub downloads get rate-limited. Injection fails only with a symlink (Steam): delete the symlink and move the XXMI Launcher folder into the game folder instead — some setups reject the symlinked path. Game won't launch via Heroic: confirm Disable umu is enabled and the Proton version is DW-Proton 11.0-5; then try other Proton builds if needed. "It worked yesterday, broke today" / weird path errors: check for a typo in a folder name — an extra space or a wrong capital letter in XXMI Launcher or the game path breaks injection silently. Wuthering Waves boots but mods don't load: make sure you copied d3dcompiler_47.dll into Wuthering Waves/Client/Binaries/Win64/ and used the SteamOS=1 launch options.
XXMI Launcher (GitHub) · Releases ProtonPlus (Flathub) Heroic Games Launcher (Flathub) ZZMI on GameBanana Creating a GitHub personal access token (classic)

Your tutorials are welcome here. You don't need to be an expert, and you don't even need to know Git — there are two ways to contribute. No Git? No problem. If you'd rather not touch the tooling, just submit your tutorial as an issue. Write the steps in plain text and we'll shape them into a proper guide — and credit you. The rest of this page is the do-it-yourself pull-request route. One-line summary (PR route): copy the template into the right category folder, fill it in, and verify it builds clean.

Pick the category folder under docs/ that matches what the thing is for (not the OS, not the date). Don't create a new category until ~2–3 guides need it. Copy the template into that folder and give it a kebab-case name: cp docs/_template.mdx docs/03-system-cli/my-new-guide.mdx Files starting with _ (like the template) are ignored by the build, so the template never shows up as a page. Fill in the front matter (title, description, tags) and each section. Make every command copy-pasteable — no unexplained placeholders; mark user values like <your-username>.

Guides are cross-platform — write the shared narrative once. Only the parts that genuinely differ per OS (install commands, file paths, etc.) go inside an <OSTabs> block, which keeps every switcher on the page in sync and remembers the reader's choice: Linux macOS Windows sudo apt install yt-dlp brew install yt-dlp winget install yt-dlp.yt-dlp Keep the tab values linux / macos / windows consistent — that's the key the sync matches on. For commands with many flags, embed an interactive generator component instead of making readers hand-edit (see src/components/).
npm run build # must finish with zero broken-link warnings npm run serve # spot-check light/dark mode and mobile width

A guide is done when every command has been actually run on the stated platform(s), it has a troubleshooting section, it's tagged, links resolve, and it renders correctly in light/dark mode at desktop and mobile widths. The full checklist lives in PROJECT.md §6 in the repo root. Happy with it? Open a pull request and thanks for contributing! 🎉
Grabbing, converting, and ripping media. Browse the guides below or in the sidebar. More planned: gallery-dl, ffmpeg recipes.

One-line summary: a batteries-included yt-dlp install — the bgutil POT provider running in Docker so YouTube stops rate-limiting you, two tuned config profiles (video + music), and a small ydl wrapper so you can type ydl music <url> and be done. This is opinionated: it reflects how I run yt-dlp. Swap the bits you disagree with. The interactive forms below bake your choices (provider port, browser cookies) straight into the commands and config.

yt-dlp is a command-line program that downloads video and audio from the web. It started as a fork of youtube-dl and has since become the de-facto tool for the job — actively maintained, faster, and with far more features. Despite the name, it isn't limited to YouTube: it supports thousands of sites (Vimeo, Twitch, SoundCloud, Twitter/X, TikTok, Bandcamp, news sites, and many more). You hand it a URL and it figures out the rest. A few things it's good at: Grab a video in the quality you want — best available, or capped at 1080p/720p, with the video and audio streams merged automatically. Rip audio only — pull an MP3 or the original audio out of a music video or podcast, no re-encoding cruft. Download whole playlists or channels in one command, skipping anything you've already fetched. Embed metadata, thumbnails, and subtitles straight into the file so your media player shows titles, cover art, and captions. Keep its own naming and folder scheme with output templates, so everything lands organized instead of as videoplayback.mp4. If you've ever wanted a local copy of something to watch offline, archive before it disappears, or listen to on a device without a browser, this is the tool. The rest of this guide gets you a setup that handles YouTube's rate-limiting and bot checks cleanly, plus a wrapper so common downloads are a single short command.

You don't need much to get going — yt-dlp itself is a single program, and the extra pieces below exist to keep YouTube downloads working smoothly rather than because yt-dlp can't run without them. Docker installed and running (install docs). This hosts the POT provider from step 2. If you've never used Docker, the short version is that it runs a small self-contained service in the background; you set it up once and forget about it. You're comfortable running commands in a terminal. Every command here is copy-pasteable, so you don't need to memorize anything — but you should know how to open a shell and paste into it.

yt-dlp is distributed for every major platform, so the install itself is a one-liner. Pick the tab for your OS. The main thing to decide is how you install it, because that determines how you'll update it later (more on that in the note below). Linux macOS Windows On Arch: sudo pacman -S yt-dlp # or, from the AUR: yay -S yt-dlp On other distros, install from your package manager or with pipx (pipx install yt-dlp). brew install yt-dlp winget install yt-dlp.yt-dlp Updating When yt-dlp is installed through a package manager, updates go through that package manager — yt-dlp's built-in -U updater won't work. If you want the self-updater, install the binary manually instead.

This is the step that makes the difference between yt-dlp "mostly working" and yt-dlp working reliably. YouTube has been tightening the screws on automated downloads, and increasingly requires a proof-of-origin token (POT) — a short-lived token that proves the request is coming from something that looks like a real browser. Without it, you'll hit "Sign in to confirm you're not a bot" errors and throttled, painfully slow downloads. Generating those tokens involves running some of YouTube's own JavaScript, which is fiddly to do by hand. The bgutil provider does it for you: it runs as a tiny background service that yt-dlp asks for a token whenever it needs one. Running it in Docker means you don't have to install its dependencies on your own system, it stays isolated, and it's always available in the background without you thinking about it. Pick the port you want it on (default 4416). A port is just the numbered "door" the service listens on; the only thing that matters is that you use the same number here and in the config later, so set it once here and the form below will carry it through: POT provider portAuto-start on boot (--restart unless-stopped) docker run --name bgutil-provider -d --init --restart unless-stopped -p 4416:4416 brainicism/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider:deno --host 127.0.0.1 The container always listens on 4416 internally; the -p <port>:4416 mapping exposes it on the host port you chose. yt-dlp talks to it at http://127.0.0.1:<port>.
documents.s: 2. Run the bgutil POT provider in Docker

documents.t: yt-dlp looks in a standard location for its config files, plugins, and the wrapper script — everything from here on lives in one folder. Creating it now means the later steps have somewhere to write to. On Linux and macOS that's ~/.config/yt-dlp; on Windows it's under %APPDATA%. This single command just makes the folder: Linux macOS Windows mkdir -p ~/.config/yt-dlp mkdir -p ~/.config/yt-dlp mkdir $env:APPDATA\yt-dlp

The Docker service from step 2 generates tokens, but yt-dlp doesn't know how to talk to it on its own — it needs a small plugin that bridges the two. Think of the service as the engine and the plugin as the wiring that connects it to yt-dlp. There's one easy-to-miss catch in how yt-dlp discovers plugins. It expects each plugin in its own named subfolder, so the files must land at plugins/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider/yt_dlp_plugins/, not directly at plugins/yt_dlp_plugins/. Get this wrong and yt-dlp silently ignores the plugin — no error, it just won't work. The commands below grab the latest release and extract it into the correct subfolder for you, so you don't have to get the nesting right by hand: Linux macOS Windows mkdir -p ~/.config/yt-dlp/plugins/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider curl -L https://github.com/Brainicism/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider/releases/latest/download/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider.zip -o /tmp/bgutil-pot.zip unzip -o /tmp/bgutil-pot.zip -d ~/.config/yt-dlp/plugins/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider mkdir -p ~/.config/yt-dlp/plugins/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider curl -L https://github.com/Brainicism/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider/releases/latest/download/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider.zip -o /tmp/bgutil-pot.zip unzip -o /tmp/bgutil-pot.zip -d ~/.config/yt-dlp/plugins/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider $dest = "$env:APPDATA\yt-dlp\plugins\bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider" New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $dest | Out-Null Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://github.com/Brainicism/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider/releases/latest/download/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider.zip" -OutFile "$env:TEMP\bgutil-pot.zip" Expand-Archive -Force -Path "$env:TEMP\bgutil-pot.zip" -DestinationPath $dest Prefer to do it by hand? Download bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider.zip from the latest release and extract it into that bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider/ subfolder yourself.

A config file is just a list of the command-line options you'd otherwise type every single time. Instead of remembering a long string of flags for quality, output naming, metadata, and the POT provider, you write them down once and yt-dlp applies them automatically on every run. This is where most of the "opinionated" part of this setup lives. We use two profiles because video and music want different defaults: config — the default profile, tuned for downloading video at a sensible quality with metadata and thumbnails embedded. config.music — an audio-only profile that strips the video stream and saves a clean audio file, for when you only want the sound. Two things to set below: the port must match the one you chose in step 2 (so yt-dlp can find the provider), and you can optionally pull cookies from your browser. Cookies let yt-dlp act as your logged-in self, which is what makes age-restricted, private, or members-only content downloadable. The files update live as you change the options: POT provider port 4416synced from step 2 Cookies from browserDon't use cookiesFirefoxChromeChromiumBraveEdgeOperaVivaldiSafariThorium (custom path) config # === Source / Auth === --extractor-args "youtubepot-bgutilhttp:base_url=http://127.0.0.1:4416" --extractor-arg "youtube:player_client=default,tv" --js-runtimes deno # === Video extraction === -f "bestvideo[height<=1440][format_note!~=upscaled]+bestaudio/bestvideo[height<=1440]+bestaudio/best" # Priority: resolution > variable bitrate > Total average bitrate > fps -S "res,vbr,tbr,fps" --remux-video "mkv/mp4" # === Output === -o "%(title)s.%(ext)s" # === Metadata === --embed-metadata --embed-thumbnail --embed-chapters --embed-subs --sub-langs "all,-live_chat" # === Network / reliability === --abort-on-unavailable-fragment --abort-on-error --no-simulate --sleep-interval 4 --max-sleep-interval 8 --concurrent-fragments 8 --throttled-rate 1M --retries 4 config.music # Prevent default config from being loaded on top of this one --ignore-config # === Source / Auth (same as default) === --extractor-args "youtubepot-bgutilhttp:base_url=http://127.0.0.1:4416" --extractor-arg "youtube:player_client=default,tv" --js-runtimes deno # === Audio extraction === -f "bestaudio" -x --audio-format best --audio-quality 0 # === Output === -o "%(title)s.%(ext)s" # === Metadata === --embed-metadata --embed-thumbnail # embed subtitle's since some audio can be hard to understand --embed-subs --sub-langs "all,-live_chat" # === Network / reliability (same as default) === --abort-on-unavailable-fragment --abort-on-error --no-simulate --sleep-interval 4 --max-sleep-interval 8 --concurrent-fragments 8 --throttled-rate 1M --retries 4 Save them into your config directory: Linux macOS Windows ~/.config/yt-dlp/config and ~/.config/yt-dlp/config.music ~/.config/yt-dlp/config and ~/.config/yt-dlp/config.music %APPDATA%\yt-dlp\config and %APPDATA%\yt-dlp\config.music The configs assume Deno The --js-runtimes deno line tells yt-dlp to solve YouTube's JS challenges with Deno. Install Deno, or delete that line if you'd rather let yt-dlp pick whatever runtime it finds. Why two files? yt-dlp only auto-loads the file literally named config. The second file (config.music) exists for the ydl wrapper in the next step, which selects a profile with --config-location. --ignore-config at the top of config.music stops the default config from being layered on top of it.

Here's the friction the two-profile setup introduces: yt-dlp only auto-loads the file named config, so to use the music profile you'd have to type out yt-dlp --config-location ~/.config/yt-dlp/config.music <url> every time. That's exactly the kind of thing you'll never remember. The ydl wrapper is a tiny script that hides that. You type ydl music <url> and it picks the music profile for you; pass a URL on its own and it falls back to the default video config. It's pure convenience — nothing here changes what yt-dlp can do, it just makes the common cases short and memorable. Save it under your config directory's bin/ folder: Linux macOS Windows Save it as ~/.config/yt-dlp/bin/ydl: Download ydl Save it as ~/.config/yt-dlp/bin/ydl: Download ydl Save it as %APPDATA%\yt-dlp\bin\ydl.bat: Download ydl.bat

Linux macOS Windows Make it executable and symlink it into ~/.local/bin (which is usually already on your PATH): chmod +x ~/.config/yt-dlp/bin/ydl ln -s ~/.config/yt-dlp/bin/ydl ~/.local/bin/ydl Make it executable and symlink it into ~/.local/bin (add that directory to your PATH in ~/.zshrc if it isn't already): chmod +x ~/.config/yt-dlp/bin/ydl ln -s ~/.config/yt-dlp/bin/ydl ~/.local/bin/ydl Add the bin folder to your user PATH (run once, then open a new terminal): [Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("Path", $env:Path + ";$env:APPDATA\yt-dlp\bin", "User")

With everything in place, it's worth confirming each piece is wired up before you rely on it. These three checks walk from the bottom up: is the provider running, does the wrapper resolve on your PATH, and does an actual download succeed end-to-end. If all three pass, you're done. The provider container is up: docker ps --filter name=bgutil-provider ydl is found and prints its usage (lists your profiles): ydl A real download works — -P sets the output directory: ydl https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dQw4w9WgXcQ -P "~/Downloads/a custom folder/" ydl music https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dQw4w9WgXcQ -P "~/Downloads/a custom folder/"

Most problems with this setup come down to one of a few things: a path that isn't where yt-dlp expects, a port mismatch between the provider and the config, or a missing dependency. Here are the ones you're most likely to hit and how to fix each: ydl: command not found — your PATH doesn't include the bin directory. On Linux/macOS confirm ~/.local/bin is on PATH (echo $PATH); on Windows open a fresh terminal after the PATH command. POT / "Sign in to confirm you're not a bot" errors — the provider isn't reachable. Check docker ps, and make sure the port in the config's base_url matches the host port you mapped in step 2. deno not found / JS runtime errors — install Deno, or remove the --js-runtimes deno line from the configs. Plugin not detected — the folder layout is wrong. You should have plugins/bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider/yt_dlp_plugins/, not plugins/yt_dlp_plugins/. Cookie errors — the selected browser must be installed and have a profile. Chromium-based browsers can lock the cookie database while running; close the browser if extraction fails.
yt-dlp · configuration docs bgutil-ytdlp-pot-provider Deno
